data.title: GAMCO Investors takes an activist stake in Golden Entertainment
data.description:
GAMCO Investors disclosed in an SEC filing that it holds 1,323,338 shares of Golden Entertainment (NASDAQ:GDEN) through various funds. The position is considered an activist stake because GAMCO noted that it could issue analyst reports, participate in interviews, or hold discussions with third parties, with Golden Entertainment (NASDAQ:GDEN) management, or with directors in which it may suggest or take a position regarding potential changes in the operations, management, or capital structure as a means of enhancing shareholder value. 

The position by GAMCO Investors represents a 5.06% stake in Golden Entertainment (NASDAQ:GDEN).

GAMCO Investors is a prominent American investment management firm based in Rye, New York, founded and led by Mario Gabelli. The firm holds positions in various companies, including Crane Company (CR), American Express (AXP), and Playa Hotels & Resorts (PLYA).

data.title: Levi's beats on earnings but gets pantsed by guidance
data.description:
Levi Strauss (NYSE:LEVI) reported another strong quarter with gains across regions, channels, segments, and categories. The company beat Wall Street’s expectations and raised its FY25 profit and organic net revenue guidance.

Unfortunately, the guidance tripped up LEVI’s expected trajectory, leaving the stock to limp into Friday’s close with a double-digit loss and end the week with a loss for the first time in four weeks.

“We think underlying demand trends remain very strong and management is simply being prudently conservative,” Needham’s Tom Nikic said, viewing Friday’s 12% drop as a buying opportunity.

A closer look at the guidance did indeed raise some red flags. First, fourth quarter revenue will look “optically worse” on tougher comparisons, leaving a gap between now and the first and second quarters of next year when Levi’s topline durability will be tested.

Second, the failure of the company to “more fulsomely” convert improved revenue trends into significant profit expansion could be amplified by tariff pressures, Morgan Stanley analyst Alex Straton said in her note to clients.

The “profitability flow-thru magnitude may have fallen short of higher buyside expectations,” Straton adds, noting the modest $0.02 increase to FY25 profit guidance.

Fourth quarter revenue growth is also likely to cause some investor concern as management now calls for organic revenue growth of just 1%, “marking a rapid deceleration,” said Wells Fargo’s Ike Boruchow.

And of course, tariffs will present a lingering headwind for companies who rely on overseas manufacturing. With the majority of their merchandise made in Vietnam and Indonesia (subject to 20% and 19% tariffs, respectively), Levi (NYSE:LEVI) may not have fully incorporated the higher expense into margin compression.

While the guidance was cited as cause for the stock’s dismal performance, pre-earnings valuation was also problematic.

“Shares can only rise so much on fundamental improvements,” said Seeking Alpha analyst Daniel Jones, investing group leader for Crude Value Insights.

“The valuation of the company is one reason why I’m downgrading it [to Hold from Buy],” Jones said, reflecting his view that any firm that relies on imported goods should be “approached cautiously.”

Agrees fellow Seeking Alpha analyst Quipus Capital.

Levi’s (NYSE:LEVI) lofty valuation is even more concerning when you consider that the denim trend is showing some exhaustion, the current consumer environment in the U.S. could weigh on store traffic, and “the fact that the company has rarely been able to surpass current operating margins” all justify profit-taking pressure off Levi’s (LEVI) recent 4-year high, Quipus Capital says.

data.title: U.S. stocks sink as renewed tariff concerns ignite volatility amid continued government shutdown
data.description:
Wall Street's major averages opened in the green, but reversed course on Friday as U.S. President Donald Trump said China has "become very hostile" sending letters to other countries wanting to "impose export controls."

This comes as the U.S. entered its tenth day of government shutdown, while a consumer sentiment survey came in unchanged, but better than expected.

The benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) was last -1.8% in afternoon trade, while the Dow (DJI) was -1.2%. The Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was last -2.4% as tech stocks tumbled after Trump threatened with massive tariffs on China.

On a Truth Social post, Trump said, "We have been contacted by other Countries who are extremely angry at this great Trade hostility, which came out of nowhere. Our relationship with China over the past six months has been a very good one, thereby making this move on Trade an even more surprising one.”

“The China trade war is getting hot. The Chinese seem unhappy about peace in the Middle East as they’ve been using it to divide our nation on their TikTok platform. Last night they began to threaten rare earth exports crucial to the US. Trump is now looking at a tougher tariff stance,” said Ross Gerber, co-Founder, president and CEO of Gerber Kawasaki Wealth and Investment Management.

The University of Michigan's U.S. consumer sentiment survey showed sentiment barely slowing to 55.0, down from 55.1 in September, and better than the 54.1 expected. Also, the 1-year inflation expectation came in at 4.6%, vs. 4.7% consensus.

In addition, the U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics said on Friday that it will publish the September 2025 CPI report by the end of the month to provide the Federal Reserve with inflation data before its next meeting.

Over in the bond market, yields were lower. The 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) was 8 basis points lower at 4.06%, while the 2-year yield (US2Y) fell 8 basis points to 3.53%.

“Fed Governor Waller is on the wires stating emphatically that the U.S. labor market is the furthest thing from being tight, wage growth is cooling which means that the supply-shortage narrative is overblown, and that all this leads to no second-round inflation impact from the tariff hikes. Just in case you’re wondering why the Treasury market is rallying,” said David Rosenberg, founder and president of Rosenberg Research & Associates.

Also within commodities, gold (XAUUSD:CUR) retreated from the $4,000 level, while silver (XAGUSD:CUR) has surpassed the $50 per ounce level. 

Wall Street's major indexes struggled to keep up momentum on Thursday and ended lower, as the U.S. Senate failed to pass two funding bills to end the government shutdown. Meanwhile, the Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell did not comment on the economic outlook or monetary policy at a Fed event.

data.title: Oil-Dri signals challenging first half and targets growth in fiscal 2026 as B2B revenue surges 24%
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Oil-Dri Corporation of America (ODC) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Daniel Jaffee, Chairman, CEO & President, emphasized the company's focus on high-value products and strategic investments, noting, "as we enter fiscal year 2026, the first 6 months will be measured against a particularly strong first half of fiscal year 2025, creating challenging comparisons. However, we remain confident in our ability to build on our past success and continue to deliver growth of our diverse product portfolio." Jaffee pointed out that while cost pressures on legacy products are intensifying due to increased asset replacement costs, cash generation remains robust, supporting dividends and future investments.
  • Susan Kreh, CFO & Chief Information Officer, reported, "Revenue increases of 10% over the fourth quarter of the prior year were driven primarily by volume, but our results also benefited from product mix improvements, which resulted in large part from the robust 24% year-over-year revenue growth in our B2B products, an excellent year for them." Kreh highlighted that full-year EBITDA reached $90 million, up 29% over the prior year, and net cash from operating activities rose 33% to $80 million. She also detailed a $33 million investment in fixed assets and noted the successful integration and performance of the Ultra Pet acquisition.
  • The management team introduced new leadership in R&D, as Dr. Mervyn de Souza outlined a vision aligned with innovation and value creation from sorbent minerals, supporting growth across divisions.

Outlook

  • Jaffee signaled that fiscal 2026 would begin with difficult comparisons due to record performance in the first half of fiscal 2025, but management expects to "deliver a year that beats last year." The company plans continued investment in business sustainability, expanded product offerings, and disciplined capital allocation.
  • Guidance language indicated the first half of fiscal 2026 will be challenging, though Oil-Dri maintains confidence in its growth plan, particularly as promotional spending in the market stabilizes and B2B momentum continues.

Financial Results

  • Kreh reported that Oil-Dri's full-year EBITDA reached $90 million, a 29% increase over $70 million the previous year. Net cash provided by operating activities was $80 million, up 33% year-over-year. Revenue increased 10% in the quarter, with B2B products achieving 24% year-over-year growth. Capital expenditures totaled just under $33 million for the year. The company paid down $11 million in debt and its revolving credit facility is currently undrawn, providing $75 million in available financing. A 16% dividend per share increase was announced for fiscal year 2026.

Q&A

  • John Bair, Ascend Wealth Advisors: Asked about Dr. Mervyn de Souza's vision for R&D. Dr. de Souza responded that his goals align with "deliver[ing] innovation and create value from sorbent minerals while helping the company grow within our existing markets and develop new products for different applications." He cited the company's integrated mining advantage and commitment to supporting all divisions.
  • Robert Smith, Center for Performance Investing: Inquired about the impact of soybean market dynamics. Bruce Patsey explained that "more soybean oil was being produced in the marketplace, and it helped drive some of our sales in the fourth quarter, and we expect that to continue in F '26."
  • Ethan Star: Asked about prospects for Amlan products and fluids purification. Wade Robey indicated that a recovery in ag markets in 2025 led to "good growth and a return to more consistent growth," with continued expansion expected in 2026, especially in Asia and Latin America.
  • John Bair: Questioned the effect of competitor promotions on clay-based litter sales. Laura Scheland clarified that the company "shifted some of our advertising spend to strategic promotional trade spend," viewing promotional activity as a long-term positive for lightweight litter.
  • Questions also addressed the integration and performance of Ultra Pet, AI adoption in R&D, and sustainability of agricultural sales growth.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts highlighted strategic questions around R&D, competitive pressures in consumer products, and the sustainability of recent sales gains, with a neutral to slightly positive tone focused on execution and future prospects.
  • Management's tone during prepared remarks was confident and focused on operational improvement and strategic investment. During Q&A, management maintained optimism, acknowledged market challenges, and provided detailed responses about specific business segments, showing careful optimism about near-term hurdles but confidence in long-term growth. Direct quotes such as "we remain very excited about our prospects for growth" (Scheland) and "we expect a good year for the business" (Robey) reinforced this.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analysts and management maintained a steady tone, with continued emphasis on overcoming short-term volatility and a strategic approach to investment and innovation.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter introduced forward guidance, highlighting expected headwinds in the first half of fiscal 2026, whereas the prior quarter focused on record-breaking results and long-term capital investments.
  • Strategic focus shifted toward leveraging B2B growth, integrating acquisitions, and expanding R&D capabilities, with more direct discussion of AI and data analytics.
  • Analysts continued to focus on competitive dynamics, product innovation, and market expansion, while management emphasized cash generation, disciplined capital deployment, and portfolio enhancement.
  • Management maintained a consistent, confident outlook, though now more explicitly cautioning about short-term comparison challenges.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management noted "margin pressure on some of these historic products" due to higher asset replacement costs and increased depreciation, with Jaffee highlighting that "the cost of those items is going up."
  • Competitive promotional activity, particularly in the lightweight litter segment, was identified as a near-term challenge, though viewed as a long-term growth driver.
  • The company acknowledged ongoing capital needs for asset replacement and the importance of maintaining strong cash flow to support investments and dividends.

Final Takeaway

Oil-Dri Corporation of America closed fiscal 2025 with strong revenue and cash flow growth, notably driven by a 24% surge in B2B product revenues and successful acquisition integration. Management signaled that the first half of fiscal 2026 will present challenging comparisons but maintained confidence in the company’s ability to deliver year-over-year growth by leveraging product mix improvements, expansion in high-margin sectors, and disciplined capital allocation. The company’s forward-looking approach, focused on innovation, AI adoption, and targeted investments, positions it to navigate margin pressures and capitalize on emerging opportunities across its diversified portfolio.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Fed's Musalem sees tariff impact on inflation fading by H2 2026
data.description:
Federal Reserve Bank of St. Louis President Alberto Musalem said Friday that inflation is still running about a full percentage point above target, though only a small portion of it, some 10%, stems from tariffs.

In any case, he expects the inflationary impact of tariffs to fade by the second half of 2026, allowing inflation to resume its convergence toward the Fed's 2% target. Meanwhile, Musalem is not ruling out more persistent inflation, or a labor market that weakens more than expected. 

Musalem reiterated that he is open-minded to future interest-rate cuts "to provide further assurance against labor-market weakening," he said during a Q&A session at a Springfield Area Chamber of Commerce event.

But, with the central bank's dual mandate of stable prices of full employment in tension, "I believe that we have to tread with caution, because there's limited room for easing before monetary policy can become overly accommodative."

The St. Luois Fed chief emphasized that a balanced approach with monetary policy only works if inflation expectations are anchored and consistent with the 2% goal. As it now stands, implied inflation is "a little elevated" at the one-, two- and three-year horizons, but long-term expectations (5-10 years out) were anchored, he noted.

The University of Michigan's consumer sentiment survey out earlier showed that inflation expectations barely budged in October.

data.title: Coca-Cola, McDonald's, and Philip Morris gain as investors turn defensive amid rising U.S.-China tension
data.description:
U.S. stocks turned lower after President Donald Trump threatened to slap a "massive increase" of tariffs on Chinese products imported into the United States to counter new export controls that China imposed on rare earths from the nation. Notably, Trump also threatened to cancel his upcoming meeting with Chinese President Xi Jinping, which adds another level of uncertainty to the trade talks and tariffs.

The tech sector led the downturn, with the Nasdaq 100 down 2.8% at 1:19 p.m. Investors seemed to be looking at the consumer staples sector as a shelter in the sudden trade storm.

The top three gainers in the Dow Jones Industrial Average were Coca-Cola (NYSE:KO), McDonald's (NYSE:MCD), and Walmart (WMT) as investors turned more cautious. In the S&P 500 Index, the two biggest gainers were PepsiCo (PEP) +3.4% and Philip Morris International (NYSE:PM) +2.7%. Consumer defensive names Campbell's Company (CPB), Dollar General (DG), Altria (MO), Mondelez International (MDLZ), and Kroger (KR) were all solidly higher.

The Consumer Staples Select Sector SPDR Fund ETF (NYSEARCA:XLP) was up 0.5% to outperform major market indexes and broad retail ETFs. XLP has been an underperformer in 2025 amid the rush into AI stocks and crypto names. The top ten holdings in the fund are Walmart (WMT), Costco (COST), Procter & Gamble (PG), Coca-Cola (NYSE:KO), Philip Morris International (NYSE:PM), Mondelez International (MDLZ), Altria Group (MO), PepsiCo (PEP), Colgate-Palmolive (CL), and Monster Beverage (MNST).

data.title: BLS will publish September CPI data by month-end
data.description:
The Department of Labor's Bureau of Labor Statistics said on Friday that it will publish the September 2025 Consumer Price Index on Oct. 24, 2025, which will provide the Federal Reserve with important inflation data before its next meeting on Oct. 28-29.

However, the agency won't resume the release of any other economic data until the government re-opens, it said in a statement. The New York Times reported that the CPI report might be released before the Fed meeting.

The CPI data is needed for the Social Security Administration to meet deadlines "to ensure the accurate and timely payment of benefits," the BLS said.

data.title: Align Tech cut to Hold at Jefferies after clear aligner survey
data.description:
Shares of Align Technology (NASDAQ:ALGN) fell ~5% on Friday after Jefferies downgraded the dental product manufacturer to Hold from Buy, citing results from a U.S. dentist survey that indicated multiple headwinds for the clear aligner maker.

Analyst Michael Sarcone pointed out that the survey conducted in early September involving 49 orthodontists and general dentists suggested that until 2030, there will be “only modest, and underwhelming, incremental clear aligner penetration.”

Additionally, the survey respondents have, on average, expected Align Tech’s (NASDAQ:ALGN) market share to drop to 70% in 2026 from 77% in 2024, with rival AngelAlign being the leading share taker. According to Jefferies, 22% of doctors have also indicated potential demand declines in the near term due to macro-related headwinds.

While Align (NASDAQ:ALGN) tech, trading at a historically low valuation, “seems relatively attractive,” the analyst expects its multiple expansion to come under pressure due to a lack of near-term catalysts and macro headwinds.  Sarcone slashed his price target on ALGN to $140 from $215 per share.

data.title: SA Asks: What's the best gold play for investors right now?
data.description:
Gold, the perennial safe haven, is once again in the spotlight, with prices hitting an all-time high of over $4,000 per ounce earlier this week.

We asked Seeking Alpha analysts Samuel Smith and Taylor Dart what they thought was the best gold play right now for investors.

Samuel Smith: To maximize gains from continued upside in the gold price, miner plays like VanEck Gold Miners ETF (NYSEARCA:GDX) and VanEck Junior Gold Miners ETF (NYSEARCA:GDXJ) are compelling choices given that an increase in gold prices has an outsized impact on their profits. 

While B2Gold (NYSE:BTG) has more than doubled since we bought it earlier this year at High Yield Investor, it remains a compelling gold mining investment opportunity, given that it is undervalued relative to many of its peers and has numerous growth catalysts (Goose ramp-up, Fekola expansion project, and attractive long-term production potential from Gramalote).

Once prepaid gold deliveries are completed by the middle of next year, Goose fully ramps up, and capital expenditures begin to subside in 2026, BTG will likely see significant free cash flow growth, assuming gold prices remain elevated. This could lead to an increase in buybacks and/or dividends, potentially catalyzing shares materially higher in the process.

Taylor Dart: Over the past year, several gold producers of varying quality have exploded off their lows given the ascent we've seen in gold prices that has resulted in exponential margin expansion. However, with money often flowing into the producers first and the developers second, there's a small handful of developers still on the sale rack. And while not all developers are created equal, as many own weaker assets and/or operate in less attractive jurisdictions, Liberty Gold (OTCQX:LGDTF) is one name that stands out as quality at a very attractive price.

The company's flagship asset is its Black Pine Project in Idaho, home to a 4.2+ million ounce and growing gold resource with attractive grades for a run-of-mine oxide heap leach operation. Following capital raises that have seen participation from Centerra Gold (CGAU) and Wheaton Precious Metals (WPM), and despite the stock's recent rally, Liberty Gold trades at a fully diluted market cap of just ~$300 million, or just 0.35x its estimated net asset value of $830 million using a conservative $3,100/oz gold price and 8% discount rate. 

At a $3,500/oz gold price, Black Pine's NAV increases to an estimated $1.14 billion, leaving Liberty Gold trading at just 0.26x NAV despite owning a Tier-1 jurisdiction project capable of producing ~183,000 ounces per year (first 5 years) at sub $1,300/oz costs with a considerable mine life (~20 years).

Importantly, this P/NAV multiple values its Goldstrike Project (gold + antimony) in Utah at zero for conservatism, with investors expected to get a spin-out of this asset over the next twelve months. So, with Liberty Gold offering nearly 200% upside to its fair value estimate of US$1.20/share and trading at barely 1x FY2030 EV/FCF estimates ($3,500/oz gold price assumption) or just 0.90x 2030 EV/FCF estimates at a $3,800/oz gold price assumption, I continue to see Liberty Gold (OTCQX:LGDTF) as a steal at current levels for patient investors looking for gold exposure without paying a lofty price, with the bonus of a spin-out in its #2 asset, Goldstrike.

data.title: Wells Fargo’s top 6 retail stocks amid slowing consumer trends
data.description:
Wells Fargo’s retailing, specialty softlines and e-commerce analytics team identified six promising stock investments despite expectations of a slower start to the fourth quarter and increasingly cautious outlook heading into the holiday season.

“With the 2H setup getting increasingly more complex, we focus on a handful of names with the most compelling idio-stories in the space,” said Ike Boruchow, senior equity consumer analyst at Wells Fargo Securities.

Kontoor Brands (KTB) emerges as the analyst’s top pick heading into 2026, positioned to weather the challenging retail environment. The recommendation comes as Wells Fargo’s traffic tracking shows September marking “one of the slowest foot traffic months of 2025,” with credit card data likewise suggesting sluggish spending to end the month, likely resulting from a combination of “weather, macro and pull-forward” factors, including the “back-to-school” sales.

Among off-price retailers, Ross Stores (ROST) stands out as the best investment play according to the research, while Tapestry (TPR) is highlighted for “riding Coach momentum.”

Boruchow noted that the Coach brand “has not run a promo for many weeks now, reflecting the strength of the brand,” with both full-price and outlet channels remaining “very clean” with “essentially no material promos” since August.

Bath & Body Works (BBWI) earned a spot on the list due to its attractive “valuation + CEO change catalyst,” while Capri Holdings Limited (CPRI) was characterized as a “misunderstood” idiosyncratic name with potential despite seeing significant downward EPS forecast revisions of 44% over the past year.

Wells Fargo’s sentiment index shows Tapestry (TPR) consistently remaining among the top four stocks year-to-date “given their strong overall performance and fundamentals.”

The RealReal (REAL) rounds out the recommendations as “the top pick among e-comm names with category momentum and AI improvements driving scale,” according to Boruchow.

Despite ongoing concerns about tariffs and macroeconomic pressures, the retail sector has demonstrated “remarkable resiliency” with positive trends year-to-date, though the analyst warned that inventory is built and “absent an [acceleration] in spend, we could be bracing for a promotional holiday season ahead.”

Here are more details of the picks:

  • Kontoor Brands (KTB) – 2025 P/E: 14.2x; Price target: $100
  • Ross Stores (ROST) – 2025 P/E: 24.1x; Price target: $175
  • Tapestry (TPR) – 2025 P/E: 22.2x; Price target: $120
  • Bath & Body Works (BBWI) – 2025 P/E: 7.4x; Price target: $44
  • Capri Holdings Limited (CPRI) – 2025 P/E: 16.7x; Price target: $25
  • RealReal (REAL) – 2025 P/E: 46.0x; Price target: $15
